source: All-In Podcast — DRAM bottleneck, AI infrastructure, Chinese open source models
summary: SpaceX IPO lockup short thesis is likely wrong due to prior liquidity access and employee buying at IPO
summary_extended: Baker pushes back on a reported hedge fund short thesis that anticipated heavy selling pressure following a SpaceX IPO lockup expiration. He argues the short thesis is structurally flawed because SpaceX employees and investors have had semi-annual liquidity windows for approximately 10 years, meaning there is little pent-up selling pressure that would typically drive post-lockup declines. He further notes that nearly half of SpaceX employees purchased shares at the IPO price, signaling strong internal conviction rather than a desire to exit, and that large holders including Baker himself are not sellers. The investment implication is that the anticipated lockup-driven selloff is unlikely to materialize, making the short thesis a poor risk-reward trade for those betting on post-IPO price weakness.
detail: Baker pushes back on a reported hedge fund short thesis predicated on lockup-driven selling. He notes SpaceX employees and investors have had semi-annual liquidity for 10 years, reducing pent-up selling pressure. Additionally, nearly half of SpaceX employees bought shares at IPO, and many large holders (including Baker) are not sellers.
confidence_evidence: Baker states this with personal conviction but acknowledges some selling is possible
quote_fragment: everybody who's a SpaceX investor or employee has had a chance to sell every 6 months for the last 10 years

ticker_audit.reasoning: The entire thesis is specifically about SpaceX's IPO lockup dynamics and the short thesis predicated on post-lockup selling pressure. SpaceX is the direct subject, though it remains private/newly public and is not a US-listed equity with a standard ticker.
